sentences.text: The first school that my parents sent was a cheder, a Jewish religious school.
I didn't like the school.
I don't recall exactly the reason.
But as far as I can recall, I didn't like the rigors of the teaching rabbis.
And I ran away.
I refused to attend that school after about a month or so.
And then my mother transferred me to a neighborhood public school, Polish public school, where I started my education.
Part of the education in a public school was-- in Poland was compulsory religious education.

And being that they did not have a Jewish religious-- there were not that many Jewish schoolchildren, or the government didn't wanted to teach that-- I attended Catholic education, the catechism and all the other things, just as a [? sitting, ?] not that I took part in it the way the kids were.
In fact, one of the teachers in the religious school was a very strict priest.
I recall him very tall, in a black, long cassock to the floor.
And he-- in the summertime just before vacation, it was very hot in Warsaw, he was always cold-- lock all the windows, and we could stifle there.
And he was, I think, sadistic.
He used to come to boys that did not answer correctly, pick them up by hair, on the side of his lock, right out of the seat.

And if you did any, any problem, or if you didn't go to mass on Sunday, he'll punish by putting a bag of dried beans he had in a corner of the classroom, and the kid had to kneel holding, 45 minutes of class, on the beans.
And I can tell you that after you got up, you couldn't walk-- so that type of education.
Also we had nuns that taught us, or taught, anyway, the-- the Christian children, rigorously, that you should not look at yourself in the mirror ever-- ever look at yourself naked in the mirror.
When you get dressed, get dressed that you don't see yourself.
It's sinful to look at yourself.
I mean, it's such a continuous, rigorous thing, but we only have to sit.
We didn't have to-- the Jewish children did not have to participate in it--
Except to listen to the-- to the anti-Semitic remarks, you know?
I think at least twice or three times a week we had religious class.
I attended, till the war, I think, five grades.
We started-- well, school started at 8 o'clock in the morning.
The classes were 45 minutes.
Then was a [? pose ?].
We sat always in the same class, and teachers came to us, not like in the United States where the student go to the teacher class.
We sat in the same-- we were assigned a class and always sat there.
There were-- the benches were for two students.
We sat in a bench, each one.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The bench opened up so you can keep your books inside the bench.
The-- we had a-- each class had a patron teacher who was responsible for the class.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]
We had to a-- a woman who-- who was teaching us Polish literature, Polish drama.
I don't recall her name.
No, no, I do.
What am I talking?
I'm not-- I remember her name, yeah-- Eliza Poliker.
Would you spell her name for  us?
Yeah, Eliza, it's E-L-I-Z-A.
Poliker is P, as Peter-- O-L-I-K-E-R.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now--
It was Eliza Poliker who changed my name, as far as I recall, from Abraham to Adam.
She-- first of all, she was Jewish.
But she said, you're not Jewish.
You're a Pole with Mosaic belief.
I don't mean mosaic, the thing-- Moses belief, OK?
And she Polenized all the Jewish boys' names, so I became Adam.
In fact, Adam has three levels.
When you're a little boy, it's Adas'-- A-D-A-S with apostrophe.
When you get to school, start school, you-- you become Adek-- A-D-E-K.
And when you are further grown up, you become Adam.
That's just a miniaturizing?
I don't know the word for it.
There is, but I don't recall it.
She was the patron of the class, so to say-- responsible for the class, as such, Eliza Poliker.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
We had classes of Polish, Polish grammar, literature.
We had math-- arithmetic, of course.
And I was very unique in it because my Hebrew teacher, who for a while lived in our apartment and was supposed to teach me religion, who was atheist, but taught me religion and Hebrew fluent.
He spoke fluent, and we spoke Hebrew to each other-- was a mathematical genius.
And he taught me algebra at the age of six.
So when I got to math and arithmetic, I was far, far ahead of anybody-- so arithmetic.
We had geography.
I remember [? in the ?] [? later-- ?] in the [? fifth ?] [? class ?] we had zoology.
We used to go to the zoo, the Warsaw Zoo on trips.
We had music, singing.
We also had handiwork.
You could choose either carpentry or metalwork or glass work classes.
--once a week.
I chose woodwork.
And we had to make-- I-- so I remember I made-- the first one that I made was a coat-hanging bar for hats and coats that you hang from with.
In fact, I can tell you that the first or second year in class they did not have yet in the school-- it was a new school.
It didn't have the classes for manual labor, [? or-- ?] [INAUDIBLE].
So they taught us-- it was, by the way, the public school was coeducational in the first grades-- taught us how to knit and how to sew.
And I know how to knit, and I know how to repair socks.
I don't know what they call that method, whatever.
We had one-- one a year, once [INAUDIBLE].
We had to sit with the girls, so we had to learn that.
They didn't have anything else.
Because while the boys were working woodwork, the girls would work on it-- girls' work, so to say-- sewing, knitting, crocheting, and so forth.
That was basically-- We also had in the fifth grade, I think, we had the first year of French-- of French that we took.
No.
I took Latin.
When I was in the Soviet Union in school, I took Latin--
--as a class, yes.
Yes.
It was we had the gym, and it was once a week, compulsory gym, which were the gym stepladders and horses and all the things that we had to attend.
No.
Did none.
No, nothing except what I mentioned before-- what my father has planned for me, not necessarily what I would wind up.
But that was the talk in the family.
A lot of things.
Well, first of all, of course, we played soccer in the streets.
Soccer is the popular game.
We played volleyball, basketball, and we did mischievous things.
Oh, for instance, we found in a basement in one of our apartments capsules with chlorine in it-- glass capsules with [? chlore ?] We took handfuls-- stole it.
And [? we ?] [? called ?] it-- handfuls, packet-fulls of it, ride on a bicycle, and throw it into stores, and they exploded.
They stunk up horribly.
We also-- oh, we did crazy things.
We took sulfur, which is yellow, and if you light it up, sulfur, it smells horrible.
We used to stick it in the keyholes in apartments, light it up, and run away.
And these-- the keyholes in Europe are very large.
It used to stunk up the apartment-- stinks, the nice things like that.
Yes, I was-- I was arrested twice.
Once for jumping trolleys, the Polish trolleys, the Warsaw trolleys, had steps on both sides of the trolley.
The-- the-- so if the trolley went one direction, they would open the door on that direction.
And then if reverse, they would close that door, open opposite.
We would jump on the steps that were closed and ride with the trolley as kids and then jump off.
There were many accidents.
Where you jump off, you may jump off onto a truck.
The police put women, policewomen, on the trolleys.
And when I was jump one time, she put a handcuff on me and locked me into the trolley so I couldn't jump off and got me out and brought me to the police station.
And then, of course, I gave them the telephone number.
They called in, and my father got me, and he was not very happy.
He almost--
I would say I was 10 [LAUGHS] or 11.
The second time was where I got arrested-- I mean, that's a juvenile arrest.
I mean, it's not arrest, [LAUGHS] I go to jail.
They let me go, of course.
When my father picked me up, they let me go-- was that when my parents, or my mother-- my father wasn't there-- on a Sunday and the maid's day out.
There was nobody home.
It was-- they-- I must have been 10, 11, something of that sort.
And we lived on the third floor.
We had a balcony.
I used to bring friends on the balcony.
We took paper bags, filled them with water, and watched for couples walking [? underneath ?], and drop the paper bags, either in front of them or sometimes on top of them.
Unfortunately, we dropped a bag on a police-- police commissioner, a-- a officer of police, and his wife or whoever.
And he ran apartment to apartment, and he caught us.
He got us-- and-- to find where it came from.
And we got arrested again-- [LAUGHS] things like that.
The first one-- the second one he wasn't home.
But the first one, he beat me up.
He said, if he has son arrested, he'll kill him again, [INAUDIBLE].
Actually, they had to separate him from-- from me.
We were fighting each other.
He was beating me up, and I was going at him.
So--
Yes.
And got me out.
No, she didn't do anything.
She didn't.
[INAUDIBLE] unless I really harm [? something. ?] [INAUDIBLE] was [INAUDIBLE].
I don't think we hit them.
I think the bag fell in front of them and splattered.
You know?
We went swimming to the Vistula River.
And it was very dangerous because that part near the Vistula River, the [INAUDIBLE] River.
I spelled it before--
--was in a poorer neighborhood, an old Polish and very anti-Semitic And we had fights all the time.
The boys, the Polish boys, had sticks with razor blades stuck on it.
We [? used to ?] get cut up.
We developed a method.
We used to go four, five, whatever boys together.
But if you went four, five, they would jump, on you'd double the amount, triple the amount-- 10 guys, 15 guys.
So we used to go two only, and the rest would be on the other side of the sidewalk.
So if they saw two, four on him jump.
So our four would jump, and we had a fight.
So at least we are on an equal basis.
Most of the time, we had to fight.
And we used to go to the river and swim there, you know, on a beach-- boys only, no girls.
Now, you mention before that you had a Hebrew teacher living with you who was giving you your Hebrew education.
Do you recall his name?
No.
Yes.
He was an older man then.
He was all alone.
He had no-- as best as I know, he had no family.
He was very well-educated.
As I say, he was a mathematical genius.
He not only taught to me, but he taught some of my cousins who-- math, who became top people.
He also was a fantastic chess player.
He played in-- in the national chess tournaments.
I-- no.
I learned chess, but I didn't play.
I wasn't good at it.
I think about two years.
He was living with us.
Then he lived with my aunt, my mother's brother's wife.
My mother's brother died of cancer before I was born.
In fact, I carry his name.
His name was Abraham.
And he lived with them.
She had two sons, and-- which I'll speak perhaps later.
And he taught the whole family Hebrew.
[INAUDIBLE] But I said he also taught religion or-- or the Bible.
But he was himself an atheist.
Yes.
I had fights all the time.
We had kids, you know, that called us names.
They would start a fight.
I fought with every one of them.
I remember once I hit a kid that-- that was-- that got me-- kicked me badly, that his head went through a little window.
They had doors with those little windows.
[INAUDIBLE] through it.
He got cut all together, but he didn't complain.
He said it was accident.
He was ashamed to-- to say that a Jewish boy knocked him out, so.

He only-- the only one-- of course, the priest, several times mentioned, you know, the killing of Christ, you know, the responsibility, the Jews, you know, the evil nations, evil people, things of that sort, you know, not paying attention there a few Jewish boys there.
But we had a French teacher and a good one.
In fact, we were forced to talk, in the first class, French.
But he would-- they had an expression in French that if you translate exactly it's not correct Polish.
And he would say, that's the way the Jew talks.
That's not Polish, you know, things of that sort.
The only thing we knew about Hitler prior of to the war-- I mean, I'm talking to a year or two year prior to the World War-- we knew of the Kristallnacht, of the pogroms that we called in Germany.
And there were-- I know of a family that run away or were expelled from Germany that came and lived in the apartment house.
There was a little boy, who spoke only German, who would join our classroom.
And we used to make fun out of him because he couldn't-- you know, the sadistic kid stuff, they are-- because he couldn't speak Polish.
But he learned very fast.
That what I personally knew of Hitler.

Later, just-- just weeks before the war, we were kept [? tolding ?] the propaganda that, of course, that the war is imminent because the German Hitler demand that the corridor between East Prussia and German through Polish territory.
And there was almost an ultimatum.
The propaganda says that we have nothing to worry about because of the Germans, everything is a ersatz, which ersatz means in German, it's artificial, synthetic, so to say.
Ersatz means synthetic, artificial-- that the tanks are paper tigers that we can defend.
And I believed it.
I was a patriot.
I believed that-- that we can hold them back.
I know of-- there were articles in Polish newspapers, anti-Nazi articles, anti-Germans.
And the government confiscated papers not trying-- because they must have been protests from Germany-- confiscated the papers just before the war.
I recall there was a poem, that is a great poem, in one of the papers.
And I still recall that poem, [NON-ENGLISH], which, if you allow me, just I'll give you a short thing.
The poem was called 'Dreams.' And it says, people have varied dreams, and it's a fantastic thing to dream.
While sometimes it's happy, sometimes sad, and sometimes a very bitter, very bad.
And he says, I saw it-- a world-- I dream of a world who believed in sin and broke-- abolished the-- the law and beauty.
And those that have shown the greatest horror were called the lords of the world.
And they all assembled.
They came and assembled on a great marketplace.
And each of them hollered, I, the greatest robber am, and I'm the greatest thief.
I'm the greatest murderer.
And I am the-- from my lips I can kill people with my words.
Each out shouting the others.
But they continuously repeated that [SPEAKING GERMAN] that Germany is over.
It's higher than that.
That was the-- the dream.
OK?
And the others, meaning the Germans, kept repeating, [SPEAKING GERMAN] We are all-- [GERMAN] means over everything.
So we are the greatest criminals, you know?
How close to the beginning of World War II do you recall reading this poem?
It was-- it was maybe a month or two.
In a newspaper, yes, in a Polish-- it was called-- the-- the paper was called [POLISH], which translated means 5:00 in morning, 5:00 AM.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was a Polish Jewish paper.
The editors were Jewish.
[BACKROUND NOISE] But it was in the Polish language.
I have not-- I don't know who was the author.
I don't recall.
But I was-- I had a-- a great memory for poetry.
I could read once, and today I can still recite poetry for hours-- Polish poetry, Russian poetry.
I read it once, and [BACKROUND NOISE] I recall it to today, the complete poem.
So I don't remember the author, no.
As I-- the only thing-- Poland was afraid to do anything, to the last minute, to antagonize Germany, so to say, because they exerted such a horrendous pressure on Poland.
So there was nothing anti-German spoken on the radio or even, as I say, in the papers.
This paper with that poetry was confiscated.
It happens that we subscribed to it.
It was delivered to us.
But in the kiosks, were all confiscated when it came out.
So there was not a public, except the last weeks was the propaganda that the war is imminent, nothing to be afraid of.
We will stop them, you know, things of that sort.
They are ersatz.
Yes.
Yes.
Yes.
Just before the war started, 1939, I remember the conversation in the family, in large, my father, in fact, wanted to get out.
He felt that there's a danger.
And there was an international exhibit, an international fair, in the United States in 1939 in September in that year in that month.
And you could obtain American visas for the-- for the fair, the international fair, without any problem, as long as you had the money to travel there.
And he wanted to go on the visa.
And if any war breaks out, then, of course, you're stuck in United States.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] As I recall it, and I was later told when we were in Soviet Union by my brother and my father, that supposedly my mother didn't wanted to leave her sister.
She didn't wanted to break the family up.
She was-- she was not saying outright no but was not ready then.
She wanted to think.
Then, of course, the war broke out, and the whole thing fell apart.
I was in Warsaw.
I remember that on the September 1-- prior to September 1, of course, there was a call for a week, two weeks before for anybody that could help to dig anti-tank ditches around Warsaw.
And, of course, we young kids, being very patriotic, we all volunteered and dug ditches and-- outside Warsaw.
They brought us by trolleys there and told us how to make gas mask out of gauze.
Yes, to-- to take gauze and put charcoal in it, and then just to cover your nose and mouth, you know.
And if there is bombing, go in a basement, things of that sort.
And 1st of September, in the morning, the German Air Force, the Luftwaffe, bombed Warsaw.
And it was the German stuka-- S-T-U-K-A-- stuka airplanes, which were dive bombers.
Dive bombers are bombers that dive down and then they release the bombs in the dive position.
But the German bombers, the stuka bombers, had-- had sirens built in into the plane.
So when they dive, they make horrible sounds that increase with the-- as closer they came to.
And they came very close to the-- almost like 100 feet over the buildings.
When release-- they release the bomb and straighten out, you could almost see the pilot.

So they-- they spread not only the bombs, which were destruction bomb, but also incendiary bombs-- phosphorous bombs-- started fires, start-- build-- destroying buildings and starting fires, but also create a terror with us with the sound, with the whistling sound.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So the war was on.
And was any destruction done immediately to your apartment house or the area near where you lived?
No.
Our apartment house went through the whole [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] German-Polish war, so to say, without being touched.
The neighboring houses, several of them, were totally destroyed-- burned and totally destroyed.
A great part of Warsaw was destroyed first by the bombers.
And then as the German army came to Warsaw and surrounded Warsaw, they used heavy artillery all night long.
The artillery would shell, and all day long, the German Air Force.
Warsaw held out for over a week, as long almost as France, before it gave in.
The Polish Army, of course, was no match for Germany.
There was mostly cavalry with swords attacking tanks.
The Germans had fun sitting on a tank and machine-gunning them.
But it held out for about a week, till there was no more water, there was no more electricity, there was no more food, and then capitulated.
We used to sit the kids-- sit on the roofs.
The roofs on Warsaw buildings were metal roofs.
And some were slated-- not flat roof-- not very steep, but some were slated.
We used to sit on a roof, and when the Germans bombed with the incendiary bombs, the phosphor bombs, they would fall.
They were canisters.
And it would takes about 20, 30 seconds before they start exploding, and they would shoot out phosphor, which would go right through the metal roof and start the fires.
The thing was that if it fell on  a roof, to throw it off into the garden, to push it off to the garden.
Luckily, our house was never bombed, so we just sat and watched them.
And I actually could see the pilots when they would dive bombing.
You could-- that how close they used to come in.
And we used to lay on the roof and watch them.
But there were kids and the other roof also.
And so that was our playground, you know, sitting on the roof.
It was exciting.
I don't-- not that I recall.
And I don't know if they-- they knew what I was doing.
[LAUGHTER]
Of course, we ran out of food.
There was a-- a small contingent of Polish cavalry stationed in the garden of our house.
Somehow or other, I don't know by what means, but a couple of the horses got killed.
We cut up the horses and have the meat-- ate the meat on it.
My mother cooked.
The-- when the Germans marched in, they marched into our--
